is a [diocese](/wiki/Diocese "Diocese") of the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England") in [North East England](/wiki/North_East_England "North East England"). The boundaries of the diocese are the historic boundaries of [County Durham](/wiki/County_Durham "County Durham"), meaning it includes the part of [Tyne and Wear](/wiki/Tyne_and_Wear "Tyne and Wear") south of the [River Tyne](/wiki/River_Tyne "River Tyne") and contemporary County Durham north of the [River Tees](/wiki/River_Tees "River Tees"). It contains 249 parishes and 292 churches. [Durham Cathedral](/wiki/Durham_Cathedral "Durham Cathedral") is the seat of the [bishop of Durham](/wiki/Bishop_of_Durham "Bishop of Durham"), and the diocesan offices are located just outside the city at Stonebridge. The bishop lives in [Bishop Auckland](/wiki/Bishop_Auckland "Bishop Auckland") and has offices in [Auckland Castle](/wiki/Auckland_Castle "Auckland Castle").

The **diocese of Durham** is a [diocese](/wiki/Diocese "Diocese") of the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England") in [North East England](/wiki/North_East_England "North East England"). The boundaries of the diocese are the historic boundaries of [County Durham](/wiki/County_Durham "County Durham"), meaning it includes the part of [Tyne and Wear](/wiki/Tyne_and_Wear "Tyne and Wear") south of the [River Tyne](/wiki/River_Tyne "River Tyne") and contemporary County Durham north of the [River Tees](/wiki/River_Tees "River Tees"). It contains 249 parishes and 292 churches. [Durham Cathedral](/wiki/Durham_Cathedral "Durham Cathedral") is the seat of the [bishop of Durham](/wiki/Bishop_of_Durham "Bishop of Durham"), and the diocesan offices are located just outside the city at Stonebridge. The bishop lives in [Bishop Auckland](/wiki/Bishop_Auckland "Bishop Auckland") and has offices in [Auckland Castle](/wiki/Auckland_Castle "Auckland Castle").

The diocese is the successor to the diocese of [Lindisfarne](/wiki/Lindisfarne "Lindisfarne"), founded in 635, which moved its seat to [Chester-le-Street](/wiki/Chester-le-Street "Chester-le-Street") in 882 and subsequently moved again to [Durham](/wiki/Durham,_England "Durham, England") in 995. Most of [Northumberland](/wiki/Northumberland "Northumberland") and the part of Tyne and Wear north of the Tyne were part of the diocese until 1882, when the [diocese of Newcastle](/wiki/Diocese_of_Newcastle "Diocese of Newcastle") was created.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Diocese_of_Durham&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Origins

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Diocese_of_Durham&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Origins")]

The line of bishops of Durham stretches back to the 10th century, when [Aldhun](/wiki/Aldhun "Aldhun"), [Bishop of Lindisfarne](/wiki/Bishop_of_Lindisfarne "Bishop of Lindisfarne") (995–1018), transferred his see to Durham around 995. The diocese was founded, with its See at [Lindisfarne](/wiki/Lindisfarne "Lindisfarne"), in 635; until the See was removed from there around 875 and translated to [Chester-le-Street](/wiki/Chester-le-Street "Chester-le-Street") ([Cuncacestre](/wiki/Cuncacestre "Cuncacestre")) in around 882.

The Bishop owes his unique position to the 7th and 8th century Kingdom of [Northumbria](/wiki/Northumbria "Northumbria"), which stretched from the [Humber](/wiki/Humber "Humber") estuary to the [Firth of Forth](/wiki/Firth_of_Forth "Firth of Forth"). Subsequently, the Kingdom came under [Danish](/wiki/Danelaw "Danelaw") and English sovereignty and was transformed into an Earldom.

When [William the Conqueror](/wiki/William_the_Conqueror "William the Conqueror") became king of England in 1066, he soon realised the need to control Northumbria to protect his kingdom from Scottish incursions. He gained the allegiance of both the Bishop of Durham and the Earl of Northumbria by confirming their privileges and acknowledging the remote independence of Northumbria.

To quell rebellions, William installed [Robert Comine](/wiki/Robert_Comine "Robert Comine"), a [Norman](/wiki/Normans "Normans") noble, as the Earl of Northumbria, but Comine and his 700 men were massacred in Durham. In revenge, the King raided Northumbria in the *[Harrying of the North](/wiki/Harrying_of_the_North "Harrying of the North")*. Aethelwine, the [Anglo-Saxon](/wiki/Anglo-Saxons "Anglo-Saxons") Bishop of Durham, tried to flee with Northumbrian treasures, but was caught and imprisoned. He later died in confinement, leaving his see vacant for William the King to appoint [Walcher](/wiki/Walcher "Walcher") as bishop of Durham in 1071.

### County Palatine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Diocese_of_Durham&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: County Palatine")]

Main article: [County Palatine of Durham](/wiki/County_Palatine_of_Durham "County Palatine of Durham")

The King also appointed [Waltheof](/wiki/Waltheof,_Earl_of_Northumbria "Waltheof, Earl of Northumbria"), an Anglo-Saxon of the old Northumbria house, as the new earl. Bishop William was on friendly terms with Earl Waltheof, who built a castle at Durham for the bishop. After another rebellion, Waltheof was executed in 1075 and in his place Walcher was appointed earl, becoming the first bishop to exercise secular authority over the area.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Walcher was well-intentioned but proved an incompetent leader. He was murdered in [Gateshead](/wiki/Gateshead "Gateshead") in 1081.

King [William Rufus](/wiki/William_Rufus "William Rufus") divided the Earldom into two parts: the lands north of the rivers [Tyne](/wiki/River_Tyne "River Tyne") and [Derwent](/wiki/River_Derwent,_North_East_England "River Derwent, North East England") were ruled by the [Earls of Northumberland](/wiki/County_of_Northumberland "County of Northumberland"), while the lands south of the rivers were put under the control of the Bishop of Durham.

The lands between the Tyne and Tees, ruled by the bishops, became known as the 'County Palatine of Durham', a defensive buffer zone between England and the Northumbria-Scottish borderland.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Due to its strategic importance and its remoteness from [London](/wiki/London "London"), the county palatine became a virtually autonomous entity, in which the bishop possessed the powers of a king. Specifically, the bishops had the authority to

* hold their own [parliaments](/wiki/Parliament "Parliament")
* raise their own armies
* appoint their own sheriffs and justices
* administer their own laws
* levy taxes and customs duties
* create fairs and markets
* issue charters
* salvage shipwrecks
* collect revenue from mines
* administer the forests
* mint their own coins

For a period [Carlisle](/wiki/Carlisle,_Cumbria "Carlisle, Cumbria") was also placed under the bishop's jurisdiction, to protect the north west of England.

Durham's exceptional status reached its zenith by 1300, when bishop [Antony Beck](/wiki/Antony_Bek_(bishop_of_Durham) "Antony Bek (bishop of Durham)") remarked that:

> There are two kings in England, namely the Lord King of England, wearing a crown in sign of his regality and the Lord Bishop of Durham wearing a mitre in place of a crown, in sign of his regality in the diocese of Durham.

To ensure that episcopal functions continued to be performed while the diocesan bishop was playing his part in political affairs of state, [suffragan bishops](/wiki/Suffragan_bishop "Suffragan bishop") were appointed. For instance, Bishop [Thomas Langley](/wiki/Thomas_Langley "Thomas Langley") served as [chancellor](/wiki/Chancellor "Chancellor") to the Kings [Henry IV](/wiki/Henry_IV_of_England "Henry IV of England"), [Henry V](/wiki/Henry_V_of_England "Henry V of England") and [Henry VI](/wiki/Henry_VI_of_England "Henry VI of England") and was frequently away in London and occasionally overseas.

### Demise

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Diocese_of_Durham&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Demise")]

In 1536 [Henry VIII](/wiki/Henry_VIII_of_England "Henry VIII of England") greatly diminished the bishop's secular authority, which was further reduced during and after the [English Civil War](/wiki/English_Civil_War "English Civil War").

From 1537 to 1572, there was one [suffragan](/wiki/Suffragan_bishop "Suffragan bishop") [Bishop of Berwick](/wiki/Bishop_of_Berwick "Bishop of Berwick"). Since 1572, the see has remained in abeyance (until 2016), and [Berwick-upon-Tweed](/wiki/Berwick-upon-Tweed "Berwick-upon-Tweed") is now in Newcastle diocese.

After the Union of the crowns of England and Scotland in 1603, the County Palatinate, founded to check Scottish incursions, increasingly became an anachronism.

The palatinate was finally abolished on 5 July 1836.[[3]](#cite_note-act-3) In 1844 the [Islandshire](/wiki/Islandshire "Islandshire") exclave was transferred to the jurisdiction of Northumberland, while the Bishop's duty to maintain a major fortress overlooking the Tweed at [Norham](/wiki/Norham "Norham") also came to an end. 1882 saw the Bishop lose the religious leadership for the whole of Northumbria when the part north of the River Tyne became the newly created [Diocese of Newcastle](/wiki/Diocese_of_Newcastle "Diocese of Newcastle"). In 1971 the [Courts Act](/wiki/Courts_Act_1971 "Courts Act 1971") modernised the English courts system and abolished the Palatinate courts.

Since 1906, there has again been a suffragan bishop in the diocese – the [Bishop of Jarrow](/wiki/Bishop_of_Jarrow "Bishop of Jarrow").

Still, people born in [Bedlington](/wiki/Bedlington "Bedlington") or the other parts of old North Durham, had birth certificates issued with the County Palatine of Durham printed on them, and the North Durham satellite areas governed their areas as Urban District Councils still under the rule of Durham. This prevailed until 1974, when administrative boundaries were changed and all of these areas, and other "autonomous" towns connected to Durham, lost their independence.

Reuse of the title: in March 2016, when announcing the retirement of the assistant Bishop of Newcastle, the new suffragan bishop, when appointed, will have the title Bishop of Berwick.

### Seals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Diocese_of_Durham&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Seals")]

To differentiate his ecclesiastical and civil functions, the bishops used two or more seals: the traditional almond-shaped seal of a cleric, and the oval seal of a nobleman. They also had a large round seal showing them seated administering justice on one side, and, on the other, armed and mounted on horseback. That design was, and still is, used by monarchs as the [Great Seal of the Realm](/wiki/Great_Seal_of_the_Realm "Great Seal of the Realm").

### Coat of arms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Diocese_of_Durham&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Coat of arms")]

As a symbol of his palatine jurisdiction, the Bishop of Durham's coat of arms was set against a crosier and a sword, instead of two crosiers, and the mitre above the coat of arms was encircled with a coronet, usually of the form known as a 'crest coronet' (and which is blazoned as a 'ducal coronet' though not actually the coronet of a duke). Although the jurisdiction was surrendered to the Crown in 1836, these heraldic symbols of their former power remain.

## Bishop's Palace

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Diocese_of_Durham&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Bishop's Palace")]

The bishop's palace is [Auckland Castle](/wiki/Auckland_Castle "Auckland Castle") in [Bishop Auckland](/wiki/Bishop_Auckland "Bishop Auckland"). Until the 1830s and the national mood at the time of the [Great Reform Act](/wiki/Great_Reform_Act "Great Reform Act"), the bishop had at least two more castles; [Norham Castle](/wiki/Norham_Castle "Norham Castle") in [Northumberland](/wiki/Northumberland "Northumberland") and his main Palace at [Durham Castle](/wiki/Durham_Castle "Durham Castle"), now occupied by [Durham University](/wiki/Durham_University "Durham University"). The bishop still has the right to use "his" suite at Durham Castle, although the right he retained to stable his horses in buildings adjacent to [Palace Green](/wiki/Palace_Green "Palace Green") in Durham has lapsed – it was noted in the preamble to the [University of Durham Act 1935](/wiki/University_of_Durham_Act_1935 "University of Durham Act 1935") ([25 & 26 Geo. 5](/wiki/25_%26_26_Geo._5 "25 & 26 Geo. 5"). c. 29) that the bishop no longer kept horses.

## Bishops

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Diocese_of_Durham&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Bishops")]

The diocesan Bishop of Durham (vacant) is supported by the [Bishop suffragan of Jarrow](/wiki/Bishop_of_Jarrow "Bishop of Jarrow"), currently [Sarah Clark](/wiki/Sarah_Clark_(bishop) "Sarah Clark (bishop)"). [Alternative episcopal oversight](/wiki/Alternative_episcopal_oversight "Alternative episcopal oversight") (for parishes in the diocese who reject the ministry of priests who are women) is provided by the [provincial episcopal visitor](/wiki/Provincial_episcopal_visitor "Provincial episcopal visitor") (PEV) the [Bishop suffragan of Beverley](/wiki/Bishop_of_Beverley "Bishop of Beverley"), [Glyn Webster](/wiki/Glyn_Webster "Glyn Webster"). He is licensed as an [honorary assistant bishop](/wiki/Honorary_assistant_bishop "Honorary assistant bishop") of the diocese in order to facilitate his work there. Besides Webster, there is also a retired honorary assistant bishops licensed in the diocese: retired [Bishop of Salisbury](/wiki/Bishop_of_Salisbury "Bishop of Salisbury") [David Stancliffe](/wiki/David_Stancliffe "David Stancliffe") who lives in [Stanhope](/wiki/Stanhope,_County_Durham "Stanhope, County Durham") since 2013 (he is also licensed in [Europe diocese](/wiki/Diocese_in_Europe "Diocese in Europe").)[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## Archdeaconries and deaneries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Diocese_of_Durham&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Archdeaconries and deaneries")]

The diocese is divided into three archdeaconries, those of [Auckland](/wiki/Archdeacon_of_Auckland "Archdeacon of Auckland"), [Durham](/wiki/Archdeacon_of_Durham "Archdeacon of Durham") and Sunderland.

See also: [Archdeacon of Auckland](/wiki/Archdeacon_of_Auckland "Archdeacon of Auckland") and [Archdeacon of Durham](/wiki/Archdeacon_of_Durham "Archdeacon of Durham")

### Archdeacons of Sunderland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Diocese_of_Durham&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Archdeacons of Sunderland")]

The archdeaconry of Sunderland was created from the Durham archdeaconry on 1 March 1997[[5]](#cite_note-5) to acknowledge Sunderland's new status as a city. The first archdeacon was [Frank White](/wiki/Frank_White_(bishop) "Frank White (bishop)") (from 1997 to 2002) who was elevated as [Bishop of Brixworth](/wiki/Bishop_of_Brixworth "Bishop of Brixworth") in 2002.[[6]](#cite_note-6) He was succeeded by [Stuart Bain](/wiki/Stuart_Bain "Stuart Bain") in 2002,[[7]](#cite_note-7) who resigned to become Provost of [Sunderland Minster](/wiki/Sunderland_Minster "Sunderland Minster") on 17 March 2018.[[8]](#cite_note-8) Bain was succeeded by [Bob Cooper](/wiki/Bob_Cooper_(priest) "Bob Cooper (priest)") on 3 July 2018[[9]](#cite_note-9) and Cooper left to become [Dean of Guildford](/wiki/Dean_of_Guildford "Dean of Guildford"), effective 26 January 2025.[[10]](#cite_note-10) [Katherine Bagnall](/w/index.php?title=Katherine_Bagnall&action=edit&redlink=1 "Katherine Bagnall (page does not exist)") was collated Archdeacon of Sunderland on 30 September 2025.[[11]](#cite_note-11)
